text: The surgeon uses a bipolar grasper to coagulate the right upper pedicles during a total laparoscopic hysterectomy. The round ligament is identified and grasped with the bipolar instrument, located approximately four centimeters lateral.

The surgeon uses bipolar coagulation to treat the round ligament and fallopian tube, ensuring the tissue does not overcook by monitoring the color and bubbling. The fallopian tube is coagulated approximately three centimeters lateral, followed by dissection with scissors. The major salpinges on the right side are visibly separated.

The external iliac artery on the right side is visibly pulsating in the triangle of dome. The surgeon completes the dissection of the mesosalpinx and proceeds to cut it. The ovarian ligament is identified and dissected close to the ovary to minimize bleeding. The major ovarian vessel is coagulated, and the surgeon continues to dissect 2.5 centimeters over the broad ligament without separating the peritoneum.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the right upper pedicle and proceeds to the left side using a ligature device. The round ligament is coagulated and cut efficiently with the ligature's cutting blade. The infundibulopelvic ligament is addressed as the ovary on this side is planned for removal. The anterior leaf of the broad ligament is opened, with the uterus pushed towards the five o'clock position and separated from five to seven o'clock. The assistant maintains the uterus in a retroverted position while adhesions with the bladder tissue are carefully separated using blunt dissection or lateralized with harmonic dissection.

The surgeon bluntly separates the bladder, revealing the vaginal fascia with a criss-cross pattern of vessels. The posterior peritoneum is visible, and the uterus is positioned at two o'clock to separate the left side. The right side posterior peritoneum is approached at ten o'clock, aligning with the arc of the uterosacral ligament. The ligature is used to coagulate ascending uterine vessels before proceeding to the uterine artery.

The surgeon coagulates the right uterine artery without cutting it, then moves to the left uterine artery, coagulating it initially and cutting it on the second attempt. There is minor venous bleeding, controlled with a ligature. The uterine artery is visibly separated medially, and the junction of the cervical vaginal fascia is placed laterally. The left side dissection is completed, with partial cutting of the Macandrot.

The surgeon performs final coagulation and cutting near the cervix on the right side, securing the uterine artery. The bladder is visibly secured, and the surgeon begins colpotomy using a full circle colpotomizer. The uterus is completely separated and pulled through the vagina, completing the procedure.

The surgeon uses a fully curved needle to perform continuous suturing of the vaginal cuff, taking bites at the uterosacral ligament corners. The needle is held with a stone holding forceps and passed through the vagina, with gloves and sponge used to prevent gas leakage. The suturing is completed with a surgeon's knot using number one vicryl, ensuring the knot is secure with assistance.

The surgeon completes the final wrap of the surgeon's knot and cuts the suture, safely removing it through a 5mm port. The surgical area is thoroughly irrigated and suctioned to check for any bleeding, revealing clear fluid with no signs of bleeding or residual tissue. The procedure concludes with repeated lavage to ensure the area is clean and free of complications.
